(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance has improved markedly from the middle of 2024:

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was an impressive $140.8 million, and capital expenditures were just $2.2 million.

The company paid a reasonable $28.4 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $128.4 million.

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $74.5 million, so the company has been repurchasing its stock at a meaningful rate.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 4.6%, a relatively low figure.

Also, the Board authorized a $50 million increase in share buybacks, with the total authorization being $300 million through the end of 2026.

Overall, the company is executing on a ‘shrink the float’ approach by using a large percentage of its free cash flow to buy its shares, increasing free cash flow and earnings per share in a shareholder-friendly focus.

Why I’m Bearish On EverCommerce

EverCommerce will likely see overall revenue contraction in 2025 due to a confluence of rebate softness, legacy product headwinds and general usage-based revenue volatility.

The company has recently acquired ZyraTalk for its AI receptionist, scheduling and dispatch capabilities, but also for EVCM’s plan to integrate its agentic AI features across its platform, adding value to other systems within its broader portfolio.

Presumably, this will enhance efficiencies and product differentiation in forward-focused clients and prospects.

While revenue may be challenged in the near term, GAAP margins have been stable with the opportunity for growth in adjusted EBITDA from its ongoing cost optimization efforts and a shift toward a higher-margin revenue mix.

Management’s current growth strategy is to increase the ‘attach rate’ of clients using its payment processing features, integrating AI and ML capabilities across its portfolio and cross-selling to increase multiple solution usage, which improves share of wallet and customer retention.

To this end, while the current net dollar retention rate is only 97%, customers using more than one solution have demonstrated an NDRR of over 100%, a positive confirming development.

I’ve created a graphic illustrating the frequency of various negative keywords used in the most recent conference call between management and analysts:

The chart indicates that the company is experiencing challenges with tariffs affecting part of its customer base and macroeconomic effects on rebates.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing EVCM at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.3x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of negative (2.8%) against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. EVCM’s NDRR of 97%, so the company has performed poorly against the benchmark.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 4.9x on October 31, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, EVCM is currently being valued by public investors at a discount to the broader index as of October 31, 2025, although the discount is less than I would have otherwise expected, likely due to its strong free cash flow generation.

Looking ahead, with a continued strong share repurchase program and the prospect for higher margins and AI integration momentum, the share price may have reached a floor, although the recent drop immediately after the earnings report likely signals investor disappointment with revenue growth stalling.

Since its IPO in 2021, the stock has mostly traded downward, and I don’t see any major forward catalyst for the stock, despite recent positive earnings growth uncoupled from its revenue outlook.

So, investor capital would probably be better deployed elsewhere.

My outlook on EVCM is downgraded to a bearish Sell.

data.title: Broadcom Q4 Earnings: 2 Accounting Details To Watch For
data.description:
AVGO stock: Q4 earnings expected on December 11

My last coverage on Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) stock was published on October 14 in an article titled "Broadcom's OpenAI Deal: You Should Consider Covered Call Options.” The coverage focused on the co-development deal with OpenAI (OPENAI) the company announced at that time. It rated the stock as a Hold and suggested the use of covered call options. Looking ahead, a key catalyst on the horizon is the company’s earnings report (ER) for Q4 2025, schedule for release on Dec 11.

Given the company’s elevated P/E ratios and also the market’s anxiety over the current state of AI investments, we feel it could be timely to share our thoughts on the upcoming ER. In particular, we will focus on two accounting details: the treatment of Restructuring and Integration Costs and also Stock-Based Compensation (SBC). As to be elaborated later, we feel these treatments could make the company’s valuation risks even higher than what’s on the surface, thus offsetting its strong business fundamentals.

As you can see from the release of Broadcom’s Q3 2025 ER below, there is a large divergence between the standard accounting and normalized earnings. The chasm between its GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) earnings and its non-GAAP “adjusted” figures is quite massive in my view. Its GAAP diluted EPS was just $0.85, while its non-GAAP EPS was nearly double at $1.69. Looking ahead to the Q4 earnings, the gap between the GAAP and normalized EPS estimates remains sizable. As seen, consensus anticipates a GAAP EPS of $1.12 and a non-GAAP EPS of $1.87.

Next, I will dissect 2 accounting details and explain why the normalized EPS may represent an overestimation of its true earnings power.

AVGO stock: Restructuring and Integration Costs

The first primary driver of Broadcom's above earnings discrepancy is related to its external acquisitions in my analyses. Broadcom has been a serial acquirer in recent years (with the recent acquisition and integration of VMware as a notable example). Due to these acquisitions, the company carries billions of dollars in "intangible assets" (like patents, customer lists, and technology) on its balance sheet and also reports large amounts of restructuring and integration costs.

To illustrate the magnitude of these costs, the chart below shows its operating expenses from its latest income statements. As seen, the company reported a total of more than $1.52 billion under “amortization of acquisition-related intangible assets” for the first 9 months of 2025 and more than $2.43 billion for the first 9 months of 2024. Similarly, the company also reported a total of about $0.44 billion under “Restructuring and other charges” for the first 9 months of 2025 and more than $1.22 billion for the first 9 months of 2024. These expenses are a significant portion of its total operating expenses (of around $13 and $14 billion for these periods).

To obtain the non-GAAP and normalized EPS, these costs were often considered "one-time" events and thus are added back to the earnings. However, given the magnitude and also the historical track record of these costs, I remain skeptical if such costs are indeed “one-time.” Especially if you subscribe to my view that external acquisition has been and will likely continue to be an integral part of the AVGO’s growth strategy. Under this view, acquisition and integration effectively become a recurring operational expense.

AVGO stock: Stock-Based Compensation

The next large adjustment on my list is SBC (Stock-Based Compensation). In its reconciliation, Broadcom excludes the cost of issuing stock to employees from its non-GAAP earnings. Technically, issuing stocks is a non-cash expense for the company. However, I believe SBC is a very real economic cost to shareholders if the issuance is sizable and also recurring.

And unfortunately, this seems to be case for AVGO judging by its cash flow statements as shown below. Also, AVGO’s SBC has shown a rapid increase over the years. The reported SBC more than tripled in the past ~2 years or so between end of 2023 and the present (from $2.17 billion in Oct 2023 to more than $6.68 as of TTM). In 2024, Broadcom reported an SBC adjustment of $5.74 billion as seen, almost equal to its reported net income of $5.89 billion.

In my model, such sizable and recurring use of SBC in essence is a transfer of value from shareholders to AVGO employees by inflating the share count. As such, from the perspective of AVGO investors, the non-GAAP EPS figures and/or the operating cash flows after the SBC adjustment represent an overestimating the company’s profitability.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of the upside risks, I am optimistic about the company’s business fundamentals and long-term growth potential for the reasons detailed in my last article. As a reflection of its growth potential, the chart below shows Broadcom’s EPS projections over the next five fiscal years. As seen, the EPS is projected to nearly quadruple during this period, starting from an estimate of $6.74 for the full fiscal year 2025 to $22.17 by FY 2029. The market expected consistent YOY growth in the range of about 26% to as high as 40% in the next few years. This massive growth potential helps to justify high valuation ratios. To wit, the forward FY1 P/E ratio for 2025 stands at more than 56x as seen.

With the anticipated annual growth rate between 26% and 40%, the implied PEG (P/E growth) ratio is around ~1.4x to ~2.0x. It is a bit on the elevated side compared to the 1x ideal threshold most GARP (growth at reasonable growth) investors seek, but it is nothing outlandish in my view given the quality of its business model.

To close, this AVGO follow-up was triggered by its upcoming ER for Q4. A closer examination of its recent financial statements and also its Q4 EPS revisions shows a sizable and persisting chasm between its GAAP and normalized earnings. Based on my analyses, I believe the chasm is mostly due to its treatment of acquisition/integration costs and also stock-based compensation. I feel these treatments are debatable in the case of the AVGO and would pay special attention to these items in the FQ4 ER.

data.title: Alphabet: Shares Rise Sharply For AI-Charged Company, But Still A Long-Term Play
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

AI has been the main theme soaking up all the limelight in 2025, and that has been generating a lot of buzz around certain companies. Primarily in the technology space, and one of those companies is Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), seeing a major increase in its Morningstar fair value estimate with our latest update. This is a more direct relation to the AI technology focus, but we also saw lifts in Caterpillar (CAT) and DTE Energy (DTE), which also noted AI being a key role for lifting price targets.

While Google's search dominance is continually under question, it continually outperforms expectations despite the calls for ChatGPT and other AI to disrupt it significantly. To be fair, it could still happen in the future; we just haven't been seeing any evidence of it yet. In fact, its latest earnings report showed that Google search "and other" was up 14.5%, helping the company hit the $100 billion revenue mark in a single quarter for the first time.

GOOGL shares have been rocketing higher this year and, in particular, the last few months. However, they are not trading over the lofty fair value estimated price, either, so taking a bit of a deeper look at this name to see if it is still a bargain. For a bit of a spoiler, it doesn't appear overly expensive for a longer-term investor who can sit pat for years instead of trying to make a quick buck.

Is GOOGL Still Cheap?

GOOGL is clearly an AI-focused company in the technology space, with its most dominant segment as search, but all the competition is expected when it comes to AI assistants. Unfortunately, or fortunately perhaps for GOOG, AI searches can be less reliable and provide inaccurate information. I know when I'm using any of them, I often find errors, but it is admittedly often on more niche or complex topics for investing.

With that said, GOOGL has been focused on their Gemini AI product and implementing that into as many applications as they can in order to gain market share in the space. ChatGPT is the leader, followed by Microsoft (MSFT) Copilot, but then we have Google's Gemini, with one site noting that Gemini is 13.4% AI search market share (both Copilot and Gemini source to this same site).

So Search isn't dying as some would expect, as that segment rose 14.5% year-over-year, and Alphabet is competing in the AI space, not throwing the towel in and giving up. With those strong results that drove GOOGL to top the $100 billion revenue per quarter mark, it sent shares higher, but being on the back of such solid results, it seemed warranted.

From Morningstar, the fair value estimate rose from $237 last month to $340 this month. More broadly, Wall Street Analysts rate shares a 'Strong Buy' with an average price target of $323.70, meaning the price is currently right around what analysts expect.

So even after an over 46% climb in the share price this year, shares of GOOGL are looking relatively cheap by analyst estimates. On the other hand, at an earnings multiple of 26x, historically, that would make GOOGL look relatively expensive. Only being pushed into that over-average P/E multiple in the last couple of months—when it was previously looking cheap.

In terms of the PEG ratio, we similarly see that trailing twelve months, the multiple was at 0.79x, but now forward PEG is now sitting at 1.75x. PEG is often another great valuation metric for faster-growing companies. A PEG ratio of 1 or higher usually indicates overvaluation—that said, with shares of GOOGL, the five-year average PEG comes in at 1.35x, so they are often regularly trading over the standard 1x that is often cited.

However, in looking at the longer term, earnings are expected to accelerate sharply this year before the earnings trajectory dips next year, but then further reaccelerates.

This is important because while the company may be at 26x now, that quickly dips down to ~25x for 2026, then ~21.5x for 2027 and then below 20x after that. For a longer-term investor, looking out a few years shouldn't be anything too difficult. I know when I enter most positions, such as Alphabet, my expectation is that I can own shares of this company forever.

In this case, you don't have to go too far out before what appears overvalued a bit now because attractively valued in the future based on growth expectations. Not to mention that in the last 12 quarters, the company has delivered an earnings surprise 11 times.

Next Dividend Aristocrat, Expecting A Long-Term Dividend Grower

Making it a bit easier to hold shares of Alphabet for investors is the dividend, though at a 0.30% yield currently, I'm sure investors aren't rushing toward it as an income investment.

On the other hand, it is expected to be a dividend grower. I believe there is a long pathway of growth that we should expect, making it a perfect dividend growth play for that slice of income investor.

They were a bit stingy with only a penny raise after initially starting a dividend, but that did still work out to a 5% increase.

Additionally, they've been funneling heavy amounts of capex into building out AI, which has negatively impacted free cash flow—which is expected to see quite a hit this fiscal year, but then ramp up quite massively from there. So, keeping a strong balance sheet arsenal and diverting FCF to capex for now appears to make more sense than necessarily getting too aggressive with dividend hikes.

A dividend aristocrat is a name that has grown its dividend for 25 years or longer. I believe that GOOGL is highly likely to be able to achieve that status. The company is in an ever-competitive field, which constantly needs reinvestment.

That said, if these reinvestments continue to deliver FCF and earnings expectations, there should be no difficultly in the company being able to both invest in itself but also send some change to its investors along the way. There is always time to grow the dividend more aggressively in the future if that is deemed to be worthwhile.

With that, though, we also shouldn't forget the buybacks. This company has spent massive amounts buying shares back as well—as most of these more mature mega-cap tech names have. These buybacks help to reduce the number of outstanding shares and help to deliver greater EPS growth.

Conclusion

With all this said, GOOGL is not looking like an extreme bargain at the current price—so we likely shouldn't be aggressive buyers at this time. An overall broader market correction could help send shares back into a bargain range. However, I think what we also see is that for an investor who has at least a couple of years of patience or even longer, even picking up some shares here and there through a dollar cost average approach can make sense.

Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) also recently added shares of Alphabet, so for what it is worth, it has a good chance of not being a terrible investment.

data.title: Dell: Don't Sit Out On The Next Rally
data.description:
Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) reported upbeat guidance going into the end of eFY26 with top-line growth expected to come in the range of $31-32b, up 32% at the midpoint when compared to the previous year’s quarter. With a robust pipeline for AI server sales, Dell may realize improved growth despite the elongated general compute server refresh. A catalyst worth considering is the transition to enterprise AI, which may drive the need for improved localized compute capacity to effectively lower the costs of inferencing, which may drive the refresh cycle in the coming year. Given the upbeat outlook, I am reiterating my Buy rating for DELL shares with a price target of $171/share at 8.31x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Dell Technologies Operations

Dell continued to experience strength for its AI server business, booking $12.3b in new orders in q3’26, largely driven by neoclouds and sovereign nations. Dell experienced some incremental margin tailwinds in the quarter, returning the adjusted gross margin back above 21%. Dell experienced a modest improvement to the operating margin as a result of lower SG&A costs in the quarter, improving the adjusted margin by 3bps.

Dell realized a 120% increase in AI server orders in q3’26, largely driven by Tier 2 cloud services providers [CSPs], sovereign nations, and enterprises. The Wall Street Journal recently reported that sovereign AI may be a major opportunity for growth as nations seek to lower their dependence on U.S. and China for developing AI capabilities. Essentially, this means that countries may expand their localized data center footprints in order to develop homegrown AI capabilities. This could be a major opportunity for Dell in the coming years as more infrastructure is needed overseas. Accordingly, global spend for services, software, and infrastructure is expected to grow by $500b to $2T in 2026 driven by the shift towards AI development.

A major factor that may impact Dell in future periods is the cost of storage. Accordingly, Micron (MU) has been curtailing NAND production for the last few quarters in order to mitigate pricing risk. In addition to this, HBM4 chips require more silicon and may eat into DRAM production over time. As a result, Micron referenced a tight supply of DRAM in Q4 ’25 which may be influencing higher memory chip prices. Though the market is relatively tight, Micron is expecting to add volumes onto the market in e2h27 at its manufacturing fab in Idaho.

In terms of traditional server sales, management indicated that Dell’s installed base of aging servers remains robust, offering significant runway for continued momentum going into eFY27 as part of the server refresh cycle. This can potentially provide some durability in earnings for the coming financial year as CPU servers offer stronger margins relative to AI servers. Given the roadmap for AI inferencing, Dell’s outlook for traditional servers may improve in the coming fiscal year as more enterprises turn to leveraging agentic AI and GenAI applications. Accordingly, the gen-17 servers are more power efficient, housing AMD EPYC or Intel (INTC) Xeon CPU processors for improved performance for AI inferencing.

One major headwind Dell may face in the coming years is the drive for more vertically integrated rack-scale solutions offered by Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). In 2025, AMD acquired ZT Systems, selling the ZT manufacturing business to Sanmina (SANM) while emphasizing a partnership for manufacturing the next generation Helios rack-scale solution.

Nvidia is also streamlining rack-scale solution offerings as part of its NVL72 stack solution, offering the full stack of solutions, including the GPUs, CPUs, NVLink, and Ethernet. Though it’s in the early stages, I believe Nvidia’s partnership with Intel Corp. to develop custom x86 CPUs to integrate in the NVLink can further drive Nvidia’s rack-scale solution offerings.

Dell Technologies Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q4 ’26, I’m forecasting Dell to generate $31.755b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $3.52/share. Modeling Dell’s financials, I’m forecasting the Infrastructure Solutions Group [ISG] segment to grow by 66% driven by an uplift in server sales paired with a sequential improvement to the operating margin.

In the Client Solutions Group [CSG], I’m forecasting continued year-over-year growth in Commercial sales offset by a moderating decline in Consumer sales. Despite the year-over-year decline in Consumer PC sales in Q3 ’26, Dell realized an 8% sequential gain, potentially suggesting an improving market, particularly as Dell adjusts its product catalog for more cost-conscious consumers. I’m expecting this trend to continue going into Q4 ’26, though to a lesser extent. As for the operating margin, I’m forecasting a 36bps improvement driven by stronger Commercial sales, particularly as enterprises modernize their workstation fleet to support localized AI inferencing.

In total, I’m forecasting a 33% increase in net revenue for Q4 ’26 when compared to the previous year’s quarter with a modest 14bps improvement to the adjusted operating margin when compared to the previous year's quarter.

Risks Related to Dell Technologies

Bull Case

Demand for infrastructure is expected to increase on a global scale in 2026 driven by increased sovereign investments in developing AI, potentially driving tailwinds for Dell AI and general compute servers. Despite the incremental marginal headwinds for AI servers, Dell may be gaining more traction in restoring its margin base, particularly as enterprises refresh aging general compute servers.

Bear Case

AI servers remain a major headwind for margins, impacting Dell’s overall performance, particularly as AI servers take up more sales mix. A slower traditional server refresh cycle may perpetuate lower margins, impacting the bottom line. Chip designers like AMD and Nvidia may streamline the rack-scale solutions process, potentially eating into Dell's market share in the coming years.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

DELL shares are currently trading near the midpoint of their historical trading range at 8.81x EV/aEBITDA, making shares relatively appealing for building up a position. DELL shares have traded down since the start of November 2025, likely the result of earnings uncertainty going into Nvidia’s Q3 ’26 release, which reaffirmed to the market that AI GPU demand remains robust.

From a tactical perspective, DELL shares are significantly oversold and appear to be reaching a turning point, particularly following the robust Q4 ’26 earnings guidance.

With an optimistic outlook for IT infrastructure going into eFY27, I am reiterating my Buy rating for DELL shares with a price target of $171/share at 8.31x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Nvidia: Potential Meta TPU Deal Is Alphabet's Gain, But Not Nvidia's Loss
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), published in August 2025, I analyzed the company’s Q2 report and explored the key takeaways, with a special focus on China. I had upgraded my rating on the stock from a Buy to a Strong Buy.

Since my article was published, the stock has gained only 0.65%, with much of the pullback happening very recently, after the company became the first to achieve $5 trillion in market cap. Nonetheless, during this period, the company underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained 4.5%.

NVDA reported its Q3 numbers last week and despite a blowout quarter, fears surrounding the AI bubble deflating, led to a significant decline in the stock (after gaining as much as 5% in extended hours). Since then, the stock has lost further ground after reports of rising competition from Alphabet (GOOGL) surfaced.

In this article, I analyze the Q3 report and explore the key takeaways. More specifically, I analyze whether one of the wildest swings in NVDA stock post its earnings from going up 5% before closing 3% down in a single trading session is justified. I also analyze whether reports of rising competition from GOOGL is justified and whether this would be a structural headwind for NVDA’s long-term growth prospects.

A Snapshot of Nvidia’s Q3 Performance

Once again, NVDA registered a strong quarterly performance, beating on both the top- and bottom-lines. More specifically, Q3 revenues came in at $57 billion, up 62% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $1.91 billion. Non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $1.30, up 67% y/y, and beating analyst estimates by $0.04. Data center revenues came in at $51.2 billion, up 25% sequentially and up 66% y/y. Gaming revenues came in at $4.3 billion, and while this was down 1% sequentially, it was up 30% y/y. Q3 gross margins, on a non-GAAP basis, came in at 73.6% and while this was up 90 bps (or 0.9%) on a sequential basis, it was down 140 bps (or 1.4%). Operating cash flows came in at $23.75 billion, which translates to a y/y growth of 34.71%.

Q4 guidance also came in very strong. More specifically, Q4 revenues are now expected to be $65 billion (+/- 2%), coming in higher than consensus estimate of $61.98 billion. Non-GAAP gross margins are expected to be 75% (+/- 0.5%). Non-GAAP operating expenses are projected to be approximately $5 billion, other income and expenses are projected to be approximately $500 million and the tax rates are expected to be 17% (+/- 1%).

The “$500 billion” mystery solved as Data Center Segment Delivers a Blowout Quarter

NVDA is one of those rare companies, which, in my view, is held accountable by both “buy-side,” and “sell-side” estimates. In other words, unlike other companies, it is never enough that NVDA beats sell-side analyst estimates, both with respect to the current quarter and the guidance. To have a great, positive quarter, it also must beat the buy-side estimate (the so-called “buy side whisper number) convincingly. I have never understood the logic behind why there is a different goalpost for NVDA, but then again, the company is the most valuable entity, in terms of market cap, on the planet for now.

Q3 was one quarter where the company did, by all estimates (buy side and sell side), deliver a “blowout” quarter. Data center revenues, which is the primary revenue generator, not only saw their growth accelerating once more, on a y/y basis, after five consecutive quarters of decline, but also delivered the highest growth in the last seven quarters on a sequential basis. It was also the first time ever that data center revenues crossed the $50 billion mark. Blackwell GPUs saw accelerated momentum, with both GB300 and the GB200 contributing “roughly two-thirds of the total Blackwell revenue.”

The other conundrum facing NVDA investors, in my opinion, was how to incorporate the forecast of $500 billion in orders through 2026, which CEO Jensen Huang had provided back in October. These orders included sales from both Blackwell and the company’s next iteration Rubin. For a company that normally provides guidance on a quarterly basis, such a forecast was not only rare but also came up with a lot of question marks in my opinion.

Not only was this forecast confirmed by the management, during the Q3 earnings call, but CFO Colette Kress also announced that this forecast didn’t include the sales from the deals announced with the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia or the deal made with Anthropic, in partnership with (MSFT). This was a much-needed clarification since it not only puts the $500 billion forecast as a floor but also proves that the demand for NVDA’s chips and GPUs is not subsiding anytime soon. And the China story is not even accounted for in both this forecast as well in my thesis, a region that is likely to start generating revenues again for the NVDA.

Potential Alphabet-Meta Partnership is Certainly a Headwind but It’s Not an Nvidia Killer

The most recent worry for NVDA investors is the potential Alphabet-Meta partnership, reported by the Information. As per the report, (META) is in talks to “spend billions” on GOOGL’s tensor processing units (TPUs). While this is certainly a headwind and has the potential to create significant competition to NVDA, the negativity surrounding the company as a result of this one report, in my opinion, is overdone, for four major reasons.

First, the report itself says that META is “considering” using GOOGL’s TPUs. In other words, no deal has been signed. Second, any potential agreement is not going to come to fruition at least until 2027. In my opinion, I don’t see it as a threat that changes the structural dynamics for NVDA. GOOGL’s TPUs are not NVDA’s only competition. There are other competitors such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), which are already eating into the market share of NVDA and who already have concrete deals for their chips. In other words, competition is already happening today and yet, NVDA did not only deliver a blowout quarter, but also announced that its demand projections are projected to be higher than the initial forecast of $500 billion.

Third, GOOGL itself is reliant on NVDA today. In other words, their own demands are not met by their own custom chips, as evidenced by the management’s commentary during the recent earnings call. More specifically, GOOGL management mentioned that they in the process of scaling their data centers using a combination of their TPUs as well as using GPUs from “our partner, NVIDIA.” Moreover, the company, at NVDA’s GTC, announced that they have started shipments of their new A4X Max Instances, which is actually powered by NVDA’s GB300.

Finally, META is not the only customer of GOOGL’s TPUs. During the earnings call, management announced that Anthropic had signed a deal with the company to “access up to 1 million TPUs.” Anthropic, a few weeks post GOOGL’s Q3 earnings release, as mentioned earlier, signed a deal with both MSFT and NVDA, under which the company would buy $30 billion of compute from MSFT’s Azure, which runs on NVDA’s systems. So META, by being in talks with GOOGL, is not setting a precedent. And unlike Anthropic, the social-media giant has still not signed a deal.

Investors, in my opinion, should not invest in NVDA because they believe that there would be zero competition. They should invest in the company, because of the progress made by the company, despite the already significant competition that is out there. While one more competitor entering the field should certainly be considered as a headwind, it won't be a game changer that causes structural damage to the AI leader, especially given the insatiable demand that is out there for AI-related chips.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Source: Author’s Ensemble Valuation Model.

To calculate the EPS for FY26, I deployed a hybrid ensemble model, which combines a traditional forecasting (regime-switching dynamic growth model) model and a machine learning model (random forest). The ensemble model captures the advantages of ARIMA models, which tend to be ideal for identifying trend and momentum and those of machine learning models, which tend to be ideal for pattern recognition.

The ensemble model, therefore, captures both NVDA’s explosive momentum as well as the historical trends of the overall semiconductor industry.

The ensemble model, as mentioned earlier, has two components:

  • Regime-Switching Dynamic Growth Model – this model helps to detect the regime change or the structural break in the EPS trend, in order to identify the point in time when the “AI Infrastructure Regime’ began (Q3 2022).
  • Recursive Random Forest Machine Learning Model – this model is a supervised machine learning algorithm, which is ideal for pattern recognition (e.g., when growth stalled after a period of margin expansion). The “recursive” component ensures that the model sticks to its predictions with each passing quarter and avoids noise. The random forest model was trained on the entire Magnificent Seven universe since this enables the model to understand how to incorporate “hyper-growth at scale.”

I undertook a walk-forward validation test for both the models, based on the most recent fourth quarters. The model metrics for each of the models are as follow:

The ensemble model was created by assigning a 60% weight to the random forest model and a 40% weight to the regime-switching dynamic growth model. The higher weight for the random forest model was because of its better MAPE. I still assigned a significant weight to the regime switching dynamic growth model to incorporate the “Super-Cycle” nature of the current AI regime, which is unique.

The price target under the Base case scenario suggests an upside of about 50% from current levels. Given that even the price target under the bear case scenario yields an upside of about 29% from current levels, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating on the stock.

There is a lot of negativity surrounding the stock at the moment, as evidenced by a weaker momentum grade (B) on Seeking Alpha’s momentum scale. As such, I would recommend a dollar cost-averaging strategy while buying this semiconductor giant.

Risk Factors

In addition to the increasing competitive landscape, which I described earlier, the other risk to my bull case is if all the infrastructure spending currently being undertaken doesn’t yield significant return on investment for the hyperscalers, which would force them to reduce their capex spending, subsequently signaling the end of the "AI Infrastructure buildout" regime. This lack of visibility on ROI and AI monetization is already being experienced across every major AI-related company (with the exception of GOOGL).

The other key risk is if the whole “circular deals,” where NVDA can be considered the ringleader given the amount of deals it has been involved in, implodes given the lack of AI monetization and ROI. This would be a significant hit to the company and would once again be a structural headwind, leading to a new regime of low growth, if any.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another blowout quarter from NVDA, with the company beating every key measurable street estimate. The data center revenues resumed their accelerating growth and the company announced that it is likely to generate more than initial forecast of $500 billion in sales of Blackwell and Rubin, announced at the DTC.

Despite a solid quarter, NVDA’s shares tumbled and took a further leg down when a new report was released, which cited that META is potentially exploring a deal with Alphabet to purchase billions in dollars of the search-giant’s TPUs. To me, while this is certainly a win for GOOGL, it doesn’t break the AI-driven investment thesis for NVDA.

The company’s competitors have already started to threaten to eat into its market share, but it keeps delivering stratospheric growth. At some point, while the increasing competitive landscape would be a headwind for NVDA, the progress it is making suggests that the company has the tools and the wherewithal to maintain its competitive moat.

From a valuation perspective, based on the results from the ensemble model that I deployed, I see considerable upside at current levels. As such, a meaningful pullback, such as the one that the company is currently experiencing, is a buying opportunity in my view.

This is not the first time that NVDA has faced a competitive threat, and it certainly won’t be the last. However, based on the most recent evidence, Jensen and Co. still have the competitive moat to thrive in the AI rat race.
